title: Clean Fuels Weekly Commentary: Credit Prices Drop WoW Amid Higher Credit Activity
content:
This Weekâ€™s Highlights

(10th â€“ 16th November 2025)
  • A total of 258,418 credits were exchanged as Type 1 trades, a rise of 31.31% week-over-week (WoW), and all non-zero trades witnessed an increase of 164.25% WoW as 1,844,418 credits were traded in CA LCFS.
  • The weekly average CA LCFS credit price stood at $53.10/MT for Type 1 transfers, recording a 2.23% fall WoW; prices of all non-zero transfers saw a rise of 1.46%, priced at $57.64/MT.
  • The average LCFS spot credit price fell by 2.18% WoW; on 18th November, the spot price stood at $51.00/MT, and the benchmark price stood at $51.50/MT.
  • D3 RIN prices rose by 1.24% WoW. D4 prices rose by 1.01% WoW, while D5 and D6 prices remained unchanged WoW.

cCarbonâ€™s Market View

The California Air Resources Board (CARB) issued a reminder that December 31, 2026 marks the phase-out date for a broad category of Tier 3 portable diesel engines. The deadline applies to small-fleet engines built before 2009 and large-fleet engines built in 2009 or later, within the 50â€“750 brake horsepower range. Last week, Oregon DEQ awarded nearly $11.5 million in Clean Truck and Infrastructure grants to support projects that cut diesel emissions and expand charging infrastructure for medium- and heavy-duty vehicles. Of 60 applications, 43 recipients were selected, including businesses, government agencies, and equipment owners, with a focus on areas most impacted by diesel pollution.

CA LCFS Weekly Price and Volume Trend

[caption id="attachment_607716" align="alignnone" width="780"] Table 1. Weekly average credit prices and weekly volumes, Source: California ARB[/caption] The average Type 1 transfer prices recorded a 2.23% WoW decrease, falling from $54.31 to $53.10 this week, and all non-zero transfer prices rose from $56.81 to $57.64, recording a rise of 1.46%. Type 1 trades witnessed 258,418 credits exchanged, recording a 31.31% rise WoW. The total transaction volumes for all non-zero trades saw an increase of 164.25% WoW, reaching 1,844,418 credits. [caption id="attachment_607717" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 1. Weekly average credit prices and weekly volumes, Source: California ARB[/caption]

CA LCFS Daily Average Spot Price

[caption id="attachment_607718" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 2. California LCFS Weekly average spot prices and benchmark (*CC.Info OTC: Numbers are an average of broker prices reported to CC.info)[/caption] Last week, the average LCFS spot credit prices fell by 2.18% WoW, reaching $52.48/MT. On 18th November, the spot price stood at $51.00/MT. The benchmark price, representing the future price of contracts expiring at the end of 2025, stood at $51.50/MT on 18th November.

LCFS Weekly Trade Frequency and Volume

[caption id="attachment_607719" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 3. Trade frequency and volume, Source: California ARB[/caption] As per the data reported by CARB last week, 40 trades were reported in the market, with 5 high-volume trades. The highest transfer by price was $70.00 each for two Type 2 transactions at volumes of 10,000 and 20,000 credits. The lowest transfer by price was $51.00 per credit for a Type 2 transaction reported for 10,000 credits. The largest volume of transfers involved 1,239,000 credits for a Type 2 transaction, priced at $58.38. The smallest volume of transfers involved 2500 credits each for three Type 1 transactions priced at $53.00, $53.25, and $54.00.

CA LCFS Monthly Volumes and Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_607720" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 4. Total monthly volumes and associated prices, Source: California ARB[/caption] The average spot Type 1 price stood at $53.62 in November so far, falling from $53.80 in October. Trade volumes for the month of November stood at 2.54M as of 16th November, 2025.

CA LCFS Weekly Volume Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_607721" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 5. Volume Price Trend indicator for the CA LCFS market, Source: California ARB[/caption] The Volume Price Trend Indicator (VPT) helps identify the parity between supply and demand for a stock. The idea behind the indicator is to multiply the market volume of LCFS credits by the percentage change in its price. If the credit price declines, the value of the indicator falls. Across 10th â€“16th November 2025, total VPT rose by 2.40% WoW, with a rise in all non-zero credit prices for the last week.

Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS): RIN Weekly Price Trend

RINs are credits used for compliance and are the â€œcurrencyâ€ of the RFS program. Renewable fuel producers generate RINs, market participants trade RINs, and obligated parties obtain and then ultimately retire RINs for the compliance list. D3 RIN prices rose by 1.24% WoW. D4 prices rose by 1.01% WoW, while D5 and D6 prices remained unchanged WoW. D3: Cellulosic biofuel D4: Biomass-based diesel D5: Advanced biofuels (produced from any type of renewable biomass except corn starch ethanol) D6: Conventional renewable biofuel ethanol derived from corn starch, or any other qualifying renewable fuel

Upcoming DatesÂ 

Key Clean Fuels Market Developments This Week

  • Oregon DEQ Awards Nearly $11.5 Million to 2025 Diesel Emissions Reduction and Infrastructure Grants Recipients: As part of its ongoing work to improve air quality, the Oregon Department of Environmental Quality announced it is awarding nearly $11.5 million in funding through its Clean Truck and Infrastructure grants. All projects focus on reducing diesel emissions from medium- and heavy-duty vehicles and/or developing charging infrastructure for those vehicles across the state and among areas overly burdened by diesel pollution. Of the 60 competitive applications the grants team reviewed, 43 grants are being awarded to Oregon businesses, government agencies and equipment owners this year.
  • Reminder for the Upcoming Phase Out Deadline for Tier 3 Engines: December 31, 2026 Phase Out Date: Large fleets were established on June 30, 2019, and have a total cumulative horsepower over 750 bhp for all portable engines under common ownership and control of the fleet. These deadlines do not apply to large fleets enrolled in the fleet averaging compliance path. These engines, whether registered in PERP, locally permitted, or otherwise subject to the Portable Diesel Engine Airborne Toxic Control Measure, will no longer be able to operate in California after December 31, 2026. The engine tier and phase out date are listed on each applicable PERP registration certificate.
  • Padilla, Schiff Announce Nearly $300 Million for Low-Emission California Commuter Buses:S. Senators Alex Padilla and Adam Schiff (both D-Calif.) announced that the Department of Transportation (DOT) awarded over $297 million to 24 California projects to help transition to low-emission bus models across the state. The funding comes through the Federal Transit Administrationâ€™s (FTA) Low or No Emission (Low-No) grant program and Grants for Buses and Bus Facilities program. Padilla and Schiff supported the Bipartisan Infrastructure Lawâ€™s substantial increase in funding for these programs. The Low-No program helps transit agencies buy or lease U.S.-made low- or no-emission buses and make critical facility, station, and charging upgrades to facilitate this transition. The Grants for Buses and Bus Facilities program provides federal funding for transit agencies to buy and rehabilitate buses and vans and build and modernize bus facilities. California will receive $267.70 million from the Low-No program and $29.68 million from the Grants for Buses and Bus Facilities program.
